# An interview with board member Zhenan Bao

The world is full of great ideas, but how do you decide which ones to follow up? Zhenan Bao of Stanford University shares her thoughts with the journal _Translational Materials Research_ , and discusses how she balances the roles of professor and entrepreneur.

## Like many prominent figures working in materials research today, you spent part of your career at Bell Labs. How did you end up there?

I joined Bell Labs right after I received my PhD. I originally applied for a post-doc position, but after the interview they offered me a position as a research scientist instead. I felt very lucky to get that, although there is a saying that ‘luck strikes people who are prepared for it’. A year before I was going to graduate I began searching for post-doc positions and at the time I saw some really interesting papers published from Bell Labs on organic transistors and organic light-emitting diodes, so I was very interested in both the fundamental and translational research work they were doing in Murray Hill, NJ, USA.

## From Bell Labs you moved to Stanford University—another institution with a strong track record in translational research. What do you think makes Stanford such a special place for pursuing new ideas?

I think Stanford is unique because of the culture of promoting innovation and creative thinking, the culture of people working together and the culture of being entrepreneurs, which is seen not just in technology transfer activities, but also in how people run their research groups. This culture was built over many years and has also influenced the numerous industries nearby in Silicon Valley.

Stanford is very much a ‘bottom-up’ institution. The administration itself doesn’t try to steer the direction of faculty members; the faculty members identify the topics that they are passionate about. I find that this ‘bottom-up’ approach comes from the students as well. We are fortunate to have really fantastic students—they want to impact on the world with the work they do.

The students here are really passionate about their work. Sometimes they may lead me to new directions that I have not thought about. These discussions motivate me to start looking into a particular area, which may eventually become a major direction for my research group.

At Stanford, what I observed is that the university tries to provide the resources and facilities to allow faculty members to pursue exciting new directions. Additionally, the campus serves to get us to collaborate and work together, for example collectively addressing complex problems that require diverse expertise.

## How do you choose which topics to explore?

It’s a mixture of several factors. We usually start a research project with a new idea that we think will have a significant impact, but at the same time this idea should align well with our overall vision of where we want our laboratory to be in the next five to ten years. The project(s) should also leverage the expertise of the group so that we can make unique contributions to the field. I believe that having longer-term goals allows us to have a better perspective to focus our groups’ efforts.

## What advice can you give junior researchers who are thinking about commercial opportunities for their work?

As a student, your priority should be to learn, grow and obtain your degree. Regardless of what you choose to do in the future, first you need to be the best scientist or engineer you can. You need to have a solid fundamental understanding that you can build on to develop the skills needed for solving problems, especially complex problems, as this will serve you well if you choose to start your own research group or technology company.

It is not a good idea to research for the sake of starting a company. If you develop something new and innovative, then some of the training associated with technology transfer often comes with it. For example, when a student develops a new material or device, we will then frequently file a patent on it. Hence, they will learn the process of how a patent works, how to file a patent and what kind of ideas can be patentable.

> We usually start a research project with a new idea that we are very excited about, but at the same time this idea or direction has to align with our overall vision and match up with where we want to be in the next five or ten years.
> 
> 

I started a company (C3 Nano, Inc.) a few years ago. What happened here was that the student who developed the invention had not actually thought about starting a company before. During the last year of his graduate work, he discovered something really exciting, which inspired his interest in starting a company. He won several entrepreneurship prizes that supported him in raising more funds to get the company started.

Today, there are a number of schemes, such as the NSF ICorp program, that will help students or post-docs who want to develop a company or a business plan based on their inventions. Stanford recently started a seed grant program to facilitate this kind of tech transfer.

## How do you manage the roles of professor and entrepreneur?

As a university professor with a start-up company there is potential for a conflict of interest, and that’s something that the university is very cautious about and monitors very closely. The way I manage is to have completely different activities in the start-up and in my research group. Of course, the goal for start-ups is usually to commercialize our invention and the problems being addressed are thus completely different from those at the university. While precautions are needed to ensure that there is no conflict of interest, and projects need to be selected very carefully for the same reasons, I think that the goals of the two responsibilities are very different. In my experience, it is something that is quite manageable, but it requires effort.

## Industrial collaboration is another vehicle for translation. What are your thoughts on sponsored research?

My group works with several companies and we also have longstanding sponsored research projects from companies. Typically, these are projects that are too far out to have immediate products, but the work is heading in very promising directions. Usually this kind of relationship is a ‘win–win’ situation. We get funding to do the research that we are excited about and at the same time, if something promising is developed, then these companies will be able to commercialize the work in the future.

Our interaction with industry also often provides valuable training for my students. It is very beneficial for them to have some interaction with researchers from industry to understand the requirements or the criteria for product success from a company perspective. They are exposed to a thinking process that is more aligned to the future development of practical technology, so I think that it is great for them to understand how that works. It also helps us to understand future trends in industry.

## At the recent MRS meeting, you gave six invited talks! Your projects include carbon for electronics, transparent conductors, energy storage devices, nano- and soft materials—how do you manage such a large research portfolio?

These different areas actually developed over time, but my group has always focused on organic and carbon nano-materials for flexible and stretchable electronics. Specifically, our group research effort is directed towards future electronics that will be more intuitive and more interactive with human beings and/or more human-like.

Over the years, we have developed new materials to enable new device concepts. As we further develop these, we discover other uses—for example, we discovered that the self-healing material we invented for electronic skin is also a great material for solving critical problems in lithium-ion battery electrodes.

This highlights one of Stanford’s greatest features, mentioned earlier—the opportunity to collaborate. When we see a potential opportunity, various faculty members and their students from the various groups get together. By working together, we’re able to leverage our developed materials to new areas and applications. Nonetheless, our group’s core has always been to focus on the development of materials, mostly organic and polymer electronic materials and carbon nanomaterials.

## Does this include graphene?

We have indeed also worked on graphene—for example, we have published work on using DNA fibers as a template for growing graphene nanoribbons, on graphene transparent electrodes and on the doping of graphene. However, we still only select areas where the interest between the organic materials and graphene really makes sense; for example, whenever we develop a great new dielectric layer for our organic transistors or a new dopant for organic transistors or conductors, then graphene is a natural material to apply the new discoveries to. That’s how we work in that area.

## Biographies

**Zhenan Bao** is a professor of chemical engineering, and by courtesy a professor of chemistry and professor of material science and engineering at Stanford University, USA. She is also a co-founder of C3 Nano, Inc. She received her PhD in chemistry from the University of Chicago, USA, in 1995 and joined the Materials Research Department of Bell Labs, Lucent Technologies, USA, becoming a ‘Distinguished Member of Technical Staff’ in 2001. Zhenan Bao has more than 350 refereed publications and more than 40 US patents. She is a founding board member of the journal _Translational Materials Research_ .
